overriding-and-specialization.label: Overriding and Specialization
overriding-and-specialization.summary: Override a parent method when a subclass needs a different result, while still reusing shared setup from the base class.

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.title: Override a label method for books
try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.prompt: You are building a small catalog app. In `models/item.py`, the base `Item` class already returns the item name from `display_name()`. In `models/book.py`, complete the `Book` subclass so it reuses the parent constructor with `super()` and overrides `display_name()` to return `"<name> by <author>"`. Keep the shared method name the same so the catalog can call it consistently.

sc-super-purpose.title: Why call super() in a subclass constructor
sc-super-purpose.prompt: Why would a subclass constructor call `super().__init__(name, price)` in a catalog app?
sc-super-purpose.hint: Think about which class already knows how to store the shared attributes.

help.concept: `super()` lets a subclass reuse the base class constructor so shared setup is not duplicated.
help.hint_1: The base class already handles common fields like `name` and `price`.
help.hint_2: The subclass can let the parent set shared attributes first, then add its own extra attributes.

options.a: To reuse the parent setup for shared attributes
options.b: To turn the subclass into a base class
options.c: To prevent the subclass from having new attributes
options.d: To make every method return the same value

fb-specialized-method-name.title: Keep the shared method name
fb-specialized-method-name.prompt: Complete the subclass method name so the service can call the same method on every item type.
fb-specialized-method-name.hint: The subclass should use the same method name as the base class method it replaces.

help.concept: For overriding to work, the subclass method must use the same name as the base class method so shared code can call it consistently.
help.hint_1: The base class method in this example is the one that returns the label shown in the catalog.
help.hint_2: Choose the method name that matches the parent method exactly, not a new custom name.

fb-super-call.title: Choose the parent setup call
fb-super-call.prompt: Fill in the missing expression that reuses the parent constructor inside a subclass `__init__`.
fb-super-call.hint: You want the parent class to handle the shared attributes first.

help.concept: Inside a subclass constructor, `super().__init__(...)` calls the base class constructor to initialize inherited state.
help.hint_1: The missing code should call the parent constructor, not assign directly to `self`.
help.hint_2: Choose the expression that starts with `super()` and passes the shared arguments into `__init__`.

mc-overriding-benefits.title: Choose the true statements about overriding
mc-overriding-benefits.prompt: Which statements are true about overriding in this topic? Choose all that apply.
mc-overriding-benefits.hint: Think about what stays the same and what changes when a subclass specializes behavior.

help.concept: Overriding keeps a shared method contract while allowing subclasses to return different results and still work in shared loops or services.
help.hint_1: Look for statements about same method names, specialized behavior, and mixed-object services.
help.hint_2: Avoid choices that claim every subclass must behave identically or that overriding removes the need for inheritance.

options.a: A subclass can keep the same method name but return a more specific result.
options.b: Overriding helps a service work with mixed object types through one shared method call.
options.c: A subclass must delete the parent method before it can specialize behavior.
options.d: Overriding means every subclass method must return exactly the same text as the base class.

dr-build-specialized-subclass.title: Order the subclass setup steps
dr-build-specialized-subclass.prompt: Put these steps in the best order for building a subclass that specializes a base class method in the catalog app.
dr-build-specialized-subclass.hint: Start by inheriting from the base class, then reuse shared setup before adding specialized behavior.

help.concept: A clean subclass usually starts by inheriting from the base class, reusing parent initialization, adding subclass state, and then overriding the needed method.
help.hint_1: The parent constructor call belongs before storing subclass-only attributes.
help.hint_2: The overridden method comes after the constructor work because it uses the stored attributes.

tokens.t1: Declare the subclass with the base class in parentheses
tokens.t4: Define the overridden method with the same name

catalog-step-book-and-movie.title: Specialize labels for two catalog subclasses
catalog-step-book-and-movie.prompt: You are extending a media catalog. The base `CatalogItem` already stores `title`. Complete both subclasses so they reuse the parent constructor and override `display_name()`: `BookItem` should return `"<title> by <author>"`, and `MovieItem` should return `"<title> (<minutes> min)"`. The workspace already includes a small `main.py` that prints both labels.
catalog-step-book-and-movie.hint: Both subclasses share the same method name, but each one formats the result differently.

help.concept: Different subclasses can override the same base method in different ways while still fitting the same base-class contract.
help.hint_1: Use `super().__init__(title)` in both subclass constructors before storing the extra field.
help.hint_2: Write one `display_name()` method in each subclass, each returning its own specialized string format.

catalog-step-sale-price.title: Override a pricing method for sale items
catalog-step-sale-price.prompt: A store app has a base `Item` class with `final_price()` that returns the regular price. Complete `SaleItem` so it reuses the parent constructor and overrides `final_price()` to apply a percentage discount. For example, a price of `50` with a discount of `20` should return `40.0`. Keep the method name `final_price()` so pricing services can call it on any item type.
catalog-step-sale-price.hint: The subclass should compute a reduced price from the inherited price and its own discount percentage.

help.concept: A subclass can override a calculation method so shared services still call the same method name while each subtype computes its own result.
help.hint_1: Store `discount_percent` in the subclass after calling `super().__init__(name, price)`.
help.hint_2: Compute the discount amount from `self.price * self.discount_percent / 100`, then subtract it from `self.price`.

catalog-step-mixed-report.title: Build a report from mixed specialized items
catalog-step-mixed-report.prompt: You now have a mixed catalog report. Complete the subclasses and the service so `services/reporting.py` defines `build_display_report(items)`, which returns a list of each object's `display_name()` result. The list may contain base `CatalogItem` objects, `BookItem` objects, and `MovieItem` objects. The service should not check types manually; it should rely on the shared method name.
catalog-step-mixed-report.hint: The service loop should trust that every object in the list supports `display_name()`.

help.concept: Polymorphic services work by calling the same method on mixed related objects, while overriding lets each subclass provide its own specialized output.
help.hint_1: Make sure each subclass overrides `display_name()` correctly before writing the service loop.
help.hint_2: In the service function, iterate through `items` and append `item.display_name()` for each object in order.

overriding-and-specialization-sketch-0.title: Why subclasses override methods
overriding-and-specialization-sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
A subclass inherits behavior from a base class, but sometimes one method needs to work differently for that specific subtype. That is when you **override** the parent method: you keep the same method name, but write a new version in the subclass.

Imagine a catalog app with a general `Item` and a special `Book`. Most items might show a plain label, but books may need the author in the label too.

```python
class Item:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def display_name(self):
        return self.name

class Book(Item):
    def __init__(self, name, author):
        super().__init__(name)
        self.author = author

    def display_name(self):
        return f"{self.name} by {self.author}"
```

Step by step:

- `Item.display_name()` returns the basic item name.
- `Book` inherits from `Item`, so it already gets `name` handling.
- `super().__init__(name)` reuses the parent setup instead of rewriting it.
- `Book.display_name()` overrides the parent version because books need a more specific result.

The important idea is that the subclass keeps the **same method contract** but provides a more specialized result.

overriding-and-specialization-sketch-1.title: Reusing parent initialization with super()
overriding-and-specialization-sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
When a subclass adds new attributes, it often still needs the base class to set up shared attributes first. That is why `super()` is useful.

```python
class Item:
    def __init__(self, name, price):
        self.name = name
        self.price = price

class DigitalItem(Item):
    def __init__(self, name, price, file_size_mb):
        super().__init__(name, price)
        self.file_size_mb = file_size_mb
```

Line by line:

- `Item.__init__` stores the shared data: `name` and `price`.
- `DigitalItem` needs those shared attributes too.
- `super().__init__(name, price)` calls the parent constructor.
- After that, `DigitalItem` adds its own attribute: `file_size_mb`.

Without `super()`, you would repeat the parent setup manually. Reusing the parent constructor keeps the code shorter and safer, especially if the base class changes later.

overriding-and-specialization-sketch-2.title: Specialized behavior still supports polymorphism
overriding-and-specialization-sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
Overriding is especially useful when a service works with a mixed list of related objects. Each object can respond to the same method name in its own way.

```python
class Item:
    def shipping_note(self):
        return "Standard shipping"

class FragileItem(Item):
    def shipping_note(self):
        return "Handle with care"

items = [Item(), FragileItem()]
for item in items:
    print(item.shipping_note())
```

What happens here:

- Both classes provide a `shipping_note()` method.
- The loop does not need separate logic for each subtype.
- A plain `Item` returns `"Standard shipping"`.
- A `FragileItem` overrides that method and returns `"Handle with care"`.

This is the start of **polymorphism**: one loop, one shared method name, different specialized results depending on the object type.

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.
